nodes.early_win_note: Outstanding tick and insect control — a flock of guinea fowl can dramatically reduce tick-borne disease risk in the settlement area.

nodes.name: Pigeons & Squab
nodes.description: Domestic pigeons (Columba livia) raised for squab meat (26-30 day old juveniles) and guano fertilizer. Pigeons are self-feeding foragers requiring minimal supplemental feed, and squab reaches market weight (350-500g dressed) in under a month. Pigeon guano is one of the richest natural fertilizers (high NPK).
nodes.early_win_note: Near-zero feed cost if allowed to forage. Squab production cycle is under 30 days. Guano is premium fertilizer.

nodes.name: Cattle Husbandry
nodes.description: Raising domesticated cattle (Bos taurus) for dairy, beef, tallow, leather, and draft power. Cattle are the largest and most productive livestock species, yielding 10-20 L milk/day at peak lactation and 275-300 kg dressed carcass. Oxen provide primary draft power for plowing and transport before mechanization.
nodes.critical_note: Cattle provide the primary draft power for pre-industrial agriculture and transport, plus dairy as the most efficient large-scale protein source

nodes.name: Sheep Husbandry
nodes.description: Raising domesticated sheep (Ovis aries) for wool, mutton, and dairy. Sheep produce 2-5 kg fleece per shearing (60-70% clean fiber yield), reach slaughter weight at 4-6 months, and provide nitrogen-rich manure (0.7% N fresh weight). Wool is the primary fiber for textiles before cotton and synthetics.
nodes.early_win_note: Sheep produce wool within the first year, enabling textile production. Fast reproduction (twins common) builds flock quickly.

nodes.name: Goat Husbandry
nodes.description: Raising domesticated goats (Capra aegagrus hircus) for milk, meat, fiber, and brush clearance. Goats are browsers that clear woody undergrowth other livestock avoid, produce milk with higher digestibility than cow milk, and thrive on marginal forage. Dual-purpose breeds provide both milk and meat efficiently.
nodes.early_win_note: Goats clear brush and browse on marginal land unsuitable for cattle or sheep, converting scrub into milk and meat. Twin kids are the norm.

nodes.name: Pig Husbandry
nodes.description: Raising domesticated pigs (Sus scrofa domesticus) for pork, lard, and waste disposal. Pigs convert feed to carcass more efficiently than cattle or sheep (70-80% dressed yield), consume food waste and crop residues other livestock reject, and produce 8-12 piglets per litter. The most productive omnivore in the livestock stable.
nodes.early_win_note: Market weight (90-110 kg) at 5-7 months. Pigs convert kitchen waste and crop residues into meat and lard, closing the food waste loop.

nodes.name: Equine Husbandry
nodes.description: Raising horses (Equus caballus) and donkeys (Equus africanus asinus) for draft power, transport, and pack carrying. Horses provide faster transport and greater pulling power; donkeys are more enduring, tolerate poor forage, and have longer working lifespans (25-30 years vs 15-20 for horses). Both species are essential for pre-industrial motive power.
nodes.critical_note: Equines and cattle together provide all pre-industrial motive power — without draft animals, agriculture and transport are limited to human labor alone

nodes.name: Camelid Husbandry
nodes.description: Raising alpacas (Vicugna pacos) and llamas (Lama glama) for fiber, pack transport, and guard duty. Alpacas produce 2-5 kg of fine fiber every 12-18 months; llamas carry 25-30% of body weight as pack animals and serve as livestock guardians against predators. Both are hardy, disease-resistant, and use communal dung piles for easy manure collection.

nodes.name: Bison Husbandry
nodes.description: Raising American bison (Bison bison) for meat and hides on grassland. Bison are naturally hardy with minimal health issues, requiring no shelter in most climates and finishing on grass alone. Their meat is leaner and higher in protein than beef. Handling requires reinforced facilities due to wild temperament and 1.8m jumping ability.

nodes.name: Rabbit Husbandry
nodes.description: Raising domesticated rabbits (Oryctolagus cuniculus) for meat, fur, and Angora wool. Rabbits produce 4-6 litters of 6-12 kits per year, reaching processing weight (2-2.5 kg) at 10-12 weeks on minimal space (0.15-0.25 m² per kg). Rabbit manure can be applied directly to crops without composting (cold manure).
nodes.early_win_note: Fastest reproduction of any livestock — a single doe can produce 100+ kg of meat per year. Minimal space, feed, and infrastructure requirements.

nodes.name: Insect Farming (BSF)
nodes.description: Farming Black Soldier Fly larvae (Hermetia illucens) for protein meal and biowaste conversion. BSF larvae convert 1 ton of organic waste into 150-200 kg of fresh larvae (40-45% protein dry weight) in 14-18 days, providing high-protein feed for chickens, fish, and pigs while reducing waste volume by 50-80%.
nodes.early_win_note: Converts food waste and manure into high-protein animal feed within 2 weeks. The fastest protein production cycle in any livestock system.

nodes.name: Soil Management & Composting
nodes.description: Systematic management of soil fertility through composting, cover cropping, and organic matter amendment. Composting converts animal manure, crop residues, and food waste into stable humus at 55-65°C, killing pathogens and weed seeds. Sustained crop yields depend on replacing nutrients removed by harvest.
nodes.critical_note: Without soil fertility management, crop yields decline 2-5% per year as nutrients are depleted, eventually making agriculture unsustainable
nodes.early_win_note: Composting animal manure and crop waste produces safe, stable fertilizer within 3-6 months, immediately improving crop yields 15-25%.

nodes.name: Vermiculture
nodes.description: Composting with Eisenia fetida (red wiggler) earthworms to produce vermicompost and worm castings. Worm castings contain 1.5-2.5% N, 1.0-1.5% P, and 1.0-1.5% K — significantly richer than thermophilic compost. Worm populations double every 2-3 months under ideal conditions, and worm tea serves as a potent liquid fertilizer.
nodes.early_win_note: Small-scale vermicomposting can be established in weeks with a bucket of worms and household food scraps, producing premium fertilizer continuously.

nodes.name: Aquaponics
nodes.description: Integrated fish and plant production system where fish waste provides nutrients for plants, and plants filter water for fish. Aquaponics uses natural biological nitrogen cycling (ammonia → nitrite → nitrate) without chemical pH adjustment, producing both fish protein and vegetables in a single recirculating system. Simpler and lower-tech than hydroponic pH control systems.

nodes.name: Electronics
nodes.description: PCB fabrication, component assembly and soldering, electrical power distribution systems, and power electronics. Bridges semiconductor device fabrication and functional electronic systems — from discrete transistor circuits to multi-chip modules and industrial power distribution. Internal organizing axis: functional (assembly of electronic systems vs. distribution of electrical power).

nodes.name: Electronics Assembly
nodes.description: PCB fabrication (single-sided through multi-layer), component placement, soldering (through-hole and surface mount), conformal coating, IC packaging (wire bonding, molding), and testing (ICT, AOI, X-ray, burn-in). Bridges semiconductor device fabrication and functional electronic systems.

nodes.name: Electrical Systems
nodes.description: Power distribution wiring, switches, connectors, fuses, circuit breakers, transformers, motor-generator sets, power electronics (rectifiers, inverters, VFDs), and electrical safety systems. The infrastructure that delivers electricity from generators to loads.

nodes.name: Semiconductor Process Chemicals
nodes.description: Photoresist chemistry (DNQ/novolak positive resist, chemically amplified resists for DUV), TMAH developer (2.38% standard), buffered oxide etch (BOE, NH₄F:HF ratios), CMP slurry (colloidal silica, alumina, Preston equation removal rates), and cleanroom chemicals (piranha etch, RCA clean SC-1/SC-2, HF dip). Ultrapure water specs: 18.2 MΩ·cm, <1 ppb TOC.

nodes.name: Clean Room Technology
nodes.description: HEPA/ULPA filtration, contamination control protocols, and purpose-engineered facility design for ultra-clean environments. A single 100 nm particle destroys a semiconductor die; modern nodes are sensitive to particles 10-100× smaller. Clean room technology is the general engineering discipline applicable to semiconductor fab, pharmaceuticals, and aerospace.

nodes.name: HEPA/ULPA Filtration & Laminar Flow
nodes.description: Filter media construction (borosilicate glass microfibers, 99.97% @ 0.3 μm for HEPA, 99.999% @ 0.12 μm for ULPA), fan-filter unit design, laminar flow principles, pre-filtration stages, and filter integrity testing (DOP/PAO challenge).

nodes.name: Contamination Control
nodes.description: Gowning protocols and garment materials, particle monitoring systems (laser scattering counters), electrostatic discharge (ESD) control (ionizers, wrist straps, conductive flooring), ISO 14644-1 classification (ISO Classes 1-9), and personnel training requirements.

nodes.name: Facility Design & HVAC
nodes.description: Positive pressure cascading (10-25 Pa), modular wall panel construction, raised perforated flooring, ceiling grid systems, make-up air handling, temperature/humidity control (22±0.5°C, 43±3% RH), and airlock pressure cascade design.

nodes.name: Quality Control & Statistical Process Control
nodes.description: Statistical process control, inspection sampling, and defect analysis for manufacturing yield optimization. Spans control charts (X-bar/R, p, c), process capability indices (Cpk/Ppk targets ≥1.33), acceptance sampling (AQL, MIL-STD-105E), FMEA, and yield modeling (Murphy's, Seeds). Semiconductor yield runs 60-90% for mature nodes and below 50% for leading edge — quality control is the difference between profit and loss. Internal organizing axis: functional (capabilities grouped by QC function: statistical monitoring, inspection, defect root-cause analysis).

nodes.name: Statistical Process Control
nodes.description: Control charts (X-bar/R for variables, p and c charts for attributes), process capability analysis (Cpk/Ppk with target ≥1.33 minimum, ≥1.67 for critical dimensions), Western Electric out-of-control detection rules, Six Sigma DMAIC methodology, and Gage R&R measurement system validation. The operating system of manufacturing quality — transforms reactive firefighting into proactive defect prevention.

nodes.name: Inspection & Sampling Plans
nodes.description: Acceptance sampling plans (MIL-STD-105E/ANSI Z1.4), AQL selection (0.10%-1.0% for semiconductor materials), incoming/in-process/final inspection procedures, switching rules (normal/tightened/reduced), and variables sampling for continuous measurements. Covers incoming material inspection (photoresist, gases, wafers), in-process monitoring (post-litho CD, post-etch, post-CMP), and wafer sort + final test.

nodes.name: Defect Analysis & Yield Modeling
nodes.description: Pareto analysis for yield-limiting defect prioritization, fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams for cause-effect mapping, FMEA with RPN risk ranking (S×O×D), root cause analysis (5 Whys, Kepner-Tregoe), and yield modeling (Murphy's model: Y=[(1-e^(-D₀A))/(D₀A)]², Seeds model). Semiconductor defect density targets: D₀ < 0.10 defects/cm² for mature nodes.

nodes.name: Vacuum Technology
nodes.description: Advanced vacuum systems for semiconductor manufacturing: roughing pumps (rotary vane, scroll, diaphragm), high-vacuum pumps (turbomolecular, diffusion), UHV pumps (cryopumps, ion pumps, NEG), vacuum chamber design (stainless steel 304L/316L, CF/ISO-KF sealing), and vacuum measurement (ionization gauges, RGA, helium leak detection). Distinct discipline from gas handling — vacuum engineering focuses on gas removal and controlled-atmosphere environments at pressures from 760 Torr to 10⁻¹¹ Torr.

nodes.name: Vacuum Pumps
nodes.description: Roughing pumps (rotary vane 10⁻³ Torr, scroll 10⁻² mbar oil-free), high-vacuum pumps (turbomolecular 10⁻¹⁰ mbar, diffusion 10⁻⁸ Torr), and UHV pumps (cryopump 10⁻¹¹ Torr, ion pump 10⁻¹¹ Torr, NEG, titanium sublimation). Pump selection matrix by semiconductor application, backing pump sizing, pump-down time calculations, and maintenance schedules.

nodes.name: Vacuum Chambers & Sealing
nodes.description: Chamber design (cylindrical, spherical, rectangular) with pressure loading and buckling analysis. Materials: SS 304L (standard), 316L (corrosive service), aluminum 6061 (lightweight). Flange systems: CF/ConFlat (UHV, 10⁻¹² Pa·m³/s leak), ISO-KF (quick-connect, roughing). Seals: OFHC copper gaskets, Viton O-rings. Viewports, gate valves, load locks, electrical feedthroughs, and virtual leak prevention.

nodes.name: Vacuum Measurement & Leak Detection
nodes.description: Pressure gauges: capacitance diaphragm (760-10⁻⁴ Torr, gas-independent), Pirani (10-10⁻³ Torr), Bayard-Alpert ion gauge (10⁻³-10⁻¹¹ Torr), Penning cold cathode (10⁻³-10⁻⁷ Torr). Residual gas analyzers (RGA, 1-200 amu) for contamination identification and leak detection. Helium mass spectrometer leak detection (10⁻¹² atm·cc/s). Pump-down curve diagnostics and RGA fingerprint interpretation.

nodes.name: Cryogenics
nodes.description: Refrigeration fundamentals, cryogenic air separation, and gas liquefaction and storage. Covers vapor-compression through Claude cycle refrigeration, double-column distillation at -196°C, expansion turbine engineering, dewar vessel design, and cryogenic insulation systems. Produces liquid and gaseous N₂, O₂, and Ar at industrial scale for semiconductor fabrication.

nodes.name: Refrigeration Fundamentals
nodes.description: Thermodynamic principles and cycle architectures for cooling from ambient to cryogenic temperatures: vapor-compression, absorption, Joule-Thomson effect, Linde cycle, Claude cycle with expansion turbines, Stirling and pulse tube cryocoolers. Provides the refrigeration knowledge base required for air separation and gas liquefaction.

nodes.name: Cryogenic Air Separation
nodes.description: Claude cycle integration with double-column distillation at -196°C, expansion turbine engineering (20,000-80,000 RPM, 80-88% isentropic efficiency), brazed aluminum plate-fin heat exchanger design, and cold box insulation. Produces high-purity N₂ (99.999%), O₂ (99.5%+), and Ar (99.999%) at industrial scale.

nodes.name: Gas Liquefaction & Storage
nodes.description: Dewar vessel design (vacuum-insulated, 0.1-3%/day evaporation losses), perlite and multi-layer insulation systems, nitrogen and helium liquefaction cycles, cryogenic transfer systems, and material selection for service below -150°C. Liquid storage provides 600-860x density advantage over compressed gas.

nodes.name: Electrochemistry & Plating
nodes.description: Semiconductor-grade electrochemical processes: copper damascene electroplating for interconnects, anodizing (aluminum Type II/III, titanium voltage-controlled oxide), electropolishing for ultrapure surfaces, electroforming for precision microstructures, and electroless plating (ENIG for PCBs). Distinct from bulk industrial electrolysis and general metal finishing by clean room requirements, sub-micron feature control, and semiconductor process integration.

nodes.name: Electroplating
nodes.description: Copper damascene electroplating for semiconductor interconnects using acid sulfate bath with accelerator/suppressor/leveler additives for bottom-up via and trench fill. Nickel, gold, and tin plating for electronics. Pulse reverse plating for sub-100 nm features. Operating at 5-40 mA/cm², 0.3-1.5V cell voltage, 20-25°C bath temperature.

nodes.name: Anodizing
nodes.description: Electrochemical oxide growth on aluminum (Type II sulfuric acid at 12-22V, 10-20 mA/cm²; Type III hard anodizing at 25-75V, 25-40 mA/cm², -1 to +5°C) and titanium (voltage-controlled color at 15-110V, 1.5-2 nm/V oxide growth). Porous AAO templates for nanostructure fabrication. Anodic bonding for MEMS packaging.

nodes.name: Electrochemical Surface Processes
nodes.description: Electropolishing (anodic dissolution at 10-40 mA/cm² in H₂SO₄/H₃PO₄ electrolyte for ultrasmooth chromium-enriched surfaces, Ra <0.1 μm), electroforming (nickel sulfamate bath for precision microstructure replication), and electroless plating (Ni-P autocatalytic deposition at 85-95°C, ENIG for PCB surface finish).

nodes.name: Copper Damascene Plating
nodes.description: Bottom-up copper fill of patterned dielectric trenches and vias using acid sulfate bath (CuSO₄ 40-100 g/L + H₂SO₄ 50-100 g/L) with SPS accelerator, PEG suppressor, and leveler additives. Pulse reverse plating at 5-40 mA/cm². Features from 25 nm to 10 μm, aspect ratios up to 10:1. Essential for all modern IC interconnect fabrication since the 250 nm node.
nodes.critical_note: Copper damascene plating is the only method to fill sub-100 nm interconnect features — PVD and CVD cannot achieve conformal coverage in high-aspect-ratio damascene structures
nodes.pinnacle_note: Sub-25 nm copper damascene requires the full semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem: clean rooms, photolithography, CMP, barrier/seed deposition, and metrology

nodes.name: Ultra-Pure Materials
nodes.description: Production of semiconductor-grade ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ·cm), 9N+ (99.9999999%) purity chemicals, and ppt-level analytical verification. Standard industrial chemistry produces 95-99.5% purity — this domain covers the 6-7 order of magnitude purity leap required for wafer processing. Multi-stage purification (sub-boiling distillation, RO, electrodeionization, UV oxidation) and contamination detection (ICP-MS, TOC analysis, particle counting) at parts-per-trillion levels.

nodes.name: Ultra-Pure Water Production
nodes.description: Multi-stage purification of water to 18.2 MΩ·cm resistivity: media filtration → activated carbon → water softening → RO (double-pass) → electrodeionization → mixed-bed ion exchange → UV oxidation (185/254 nm) → sub-micron ultrafiltration → cleanroom distribution in PFA piping. TOC <1 ppb, particles <0.05 μm, dissolved oxygen <5 ppb, total metals <1 ppt.

nodes.name: High-Purity Chemical Purification
nodes.description: Purification of semiconductor process chemicals to 9N+ (99.9999999%) purity: sub-boiling distillation in PTFE/quartz apparatus, isothermal distillation, ion exchange, and zone refining. Electronic-grade HF (<10 ppt metals), H₂O₂ (<100 ppt), HCl, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, IPA, and NH₄OH. Cleanroom packaging in PFA containers under ISO 4 conditions.

nodes.name: PPT-Level Analytical Verification
nodes.description: Contamination detection and quantification at parts-per-trillion (ppt) levels: ICP-MS for metallic impurities (0.1-10 ppt detection), TOC analysis (<0.5 ppb), ion chromatography for anions/cations (<0.01 ppb), laser particle counting (0.05 μm), and dissolved oxygen monitoring (<0.1 ppb). Quality assurance framework with SEMI standard methods for electronic-grade chemical certification.

nodes.name: Precision Motion Control
nodes.description: Nanometer-precision positioning, wafer stages, vibration isolation, and encoder feedback systems for semiconductor lithography and metrology equipment. Distinct from machine tools (which shape material) — precision motion controls position with nanometer accuracy using piezo actuators, air bearings, linear motors, laser interferometers, and active vibration cancellation. Organized by precision ladder: from micrometer linear stages to sub-5 nm wafer scanner positioning.

nodes.name: Nanometer Positioning
nodes.description: Sub-micron positioning systems using piezoelectric stack actuators (0.01 nm resolution), air bearing slides (0.01 μm straightness), ironless linear motors (zero cogging), and voice coil actuators. Provides the actuation layer for semiconductor lithography, inspection, and metrology. Includes flexure-guided stages, piezo-walk drives for long travel, and magnetic levitation bearings for vacuum environments.

nodes.name: Wafer Stages & Scanner Systems
nodes.description: Stepper and scanner wafer positioning stages for photolithography: 6-DOF coarse-fine architecture, dual-stage scanning at 1-2 m/s with ±5 nm positioning accuracy, reticle stage synchronization to <1 nm error, and vacuum-compatible magnetic levitation for EUV lithography. The most mechanically demanding assembly in semiconductor manufacturing.
nodes.critical_note: Without nanometer-accurate wafer stages, photolithography cannot achieve overlay precision needed for sub-100 nm IC fabrication
nodes.pinnacle_note: EUV scanner wafer stages with magnetic levitation in vacuum at ±5 nm require the full semiconductor equipment ecosystem

nodes.name: Vibration Isolation
nodes.description: Passive pneumatic isolation (0.5-3 Hz natural frequency), active feedback cancellation with voice coil actuators and geophone sensors, foundation inertia blocks, and VC-curve vibration specifications for semiconductor fabs. Creates the ultra-quiet mechanical environment required for nanometer-precision work by attenuating floor vibration from machinery, traffic, and HVAC systems.

nodes.name: Precision Encoders & Feedback
nodes.description: Laser interferometers (0.15-0.6 nm resolution with heterodyne detection), optical scale encoders (0.24 nm with holographic gratings and interpolation), capacitive displacement sensors, and encoder signal processing. Provides the position measurement backbone for closed-loop nanometer positioning in wafer stages and metrology equipment.

nodes.name: Petroleum Extraction & Refining
nodes.description: Crude oil extraction (surface seeps through rotary drilling), petroleum refining (atmospheric and vacuum distillation, catalytic cracking, hydrocracking, reforming), and petrochemical feedstock production (ethylene, propylene, BTX aromatics). Organized by process chain from wellhead to chemical building blocks.

nodes.name: Crude Oil Extraction
nodes.description: Surface seep collection, cable-tool percussion drilling, rotary drilling with mud circulation, well completion, artificial lift, and enhanced oil recovery. Produces crude oil and associated natural gas from subsurface reservoirs.

nodes.name: Cable-Tool Drilling
nodes.description: Percussion drilling using a heavy steel bit repeatedly lifted and dropped onto the rock. Depth range 50-1,500 m, penetration rate 0.5-10 m/day. Requires steam engine or animal power for the walking beam. Used for the first commercial oil wells (Titusville, 1859).

nodes.name: Rotary Drilling
nodes.description: Rotating drill bit with continuous drilling mud circulation. Depth range 100-5,000+ m, penetration rate 10-100 m/day. Requires steel drill pipe, mud pumps, and blowout preventer. The dominant drilling method since the 1920s.

nodes.name: Petroleum Refining
nodes.description: Atmospheric distillation (ADU), vacuum distillation (VDU), catalytic cracking (FCC), hydrocracking, catalytic reforming, hydrotreating, and product blending. Converts crude oil into gasoline, kerosene, diesel, fuel oil, lubricants, and asphalt.

nodes.name: Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation
nodes.description: ADU separates crude into naphtha (40-180°C), kerosene (180-260°C), diesel (260-350°C), and atmospheric residue. VDU processes residue at 10-30 mmHg to produce vacuum gas oil and vacuum residue. Together they provide the primary separation that all downstream units depend on.

nodes.name: Catalytic Cracking & Conversion
nodes.description: FCC converts heavy gas oil to gasoline (45-60% yield) and propylene using zeolite Y catalyst at 500-550°C. Hydrocracking converts VGO to diesel and jet fuel at 350-430°C and 80-200 bar with hydrogen. Reforming produces BTX aromatics and hydrogen from naphtha at 490-530°C over Pt-Re catalyst.

nodes.name: Petrochemical Feedstocks
nodes.description: Steam cracking for ethylene and propylene from naphtha or ethane feedstock, BTX aromatics extraction from reformate, and downstream monomer production (styrene, ethylene oxide, acrylonitrile). Provides the building blocks for polymers, solvents, and specialty chemicals.

nodes.name: Steam Cracking (Olefin Production)
nodes.description: Naphtha or ethane mixed with steam and heated to 750-900°C in tubular furnace for 0.1-0.5 seconds residence time. Free-radical cracking produces ethylene (30-84% yield depending on feedstock) and propylene. Cryogenic separation train yields polymer-grade ethylene (99.9%) and propylene (99.5%+).

nodes.name: Economics & Organization
nodes.description: The social and organizational infrastructure for coordinating production, trade, labor specialization, and resource allocation. Enables complex multi-specialist production beyond household-level autarky. Spans division of labor, exchange systems, currency, accounting, supply chain coordination, and governance structures.
nodes.critical_note: Without organizational coordination, no complex multi-specialist technology (from blast furnaces to semiconductor fabs) is achievable
nodes.early_win_note: Division of labor and trade create the earliest and most powerful positive feedback loop: surplus to specialization to exchange to further specialization

nodes.name: Division of Labor
nodes.description: Specialization of workers into distinct trades and coordination of their interdependent tasks. Emerges directly from food surplus: when not everyone must farm, specialists can focus on metallurgy, pottery, construction, or tool-making. The foundational organizational technology.
nodes.critical_note: Every downstream capability from metals to semiconductors implicitly requires specialist workers
nodes.early_win_note: Surplus to specialization to exchange feedback loop: each specialist produces more than a generalist could

nodes.name: Trade & Barter
nodes.description: Exchange of surplus goods between specialists through barter and market systems. Enables each specialist to obtain goods they do not produce directly, creating the economic feedback loop: specialization to surplus to exchange to further specialization.
nodes.early_win_note: Trade enables surplus circulation, dramatically increasing the value of specialization

nodes.name: Currency & Standardized Exchange
nodes.description: Standardized exchange media (commodity money, metal coinage) and units of account that solve barter's double-coincidence problem. Requires reliable metallurgy for coinage and standardized weights for valuation.

nodes.name: Accounting & Record-Keeping
nodes.description: Systematic recording of production inputs, trade transactions, and resource inventories. Emerges when the volume of economic activity exceeds what can be tracked mentally, roughly when a community supports more than 50 specialists.

nodes.name: Supply Chain Coordination
nodes.description: Planning and management of multi-step production sequences, procurement of inputs from diverse sources, and inventory management across distributed operations. Essential when production involves more than 3 sequential specialist steps.
nodes.critical_note: Semiconductor manufacturing is impossible without multi-continent supply chain coordination involving hundreds of specialist inputs

nodes.name: Governance & Institutional Design
nodes.description: Decision-making structures for large-scale resource allocation, dispute resolution, and collective action. Includes guild structures, cooperative governance, and administrative hierarchies that coordinate hundreds of workers.

nodes.name: Environmental Health & Safety
nodes.description: Semiconductor-specific chemical safety, toxicology, ventilation engineering, PPE systems, emergency response, and waste management for hazardous process chemicals (HF, silane, arsine, phosphine). Distinct from general occupational health by focus on ultra-hazardous hydride gases, pyrophoric materials, and semiconductor waste streams. Organized by functional safety domain: chemical hazards, ventilation, PPE, emergency response, waste treatment.

nodes.name: Chemical Safety & Toxicology
nodes.description: Hazard classification, TLV/IDLH exposure limits, NFPA 704 diamond ratings, gas compatibility matrices, and chemical-specific safety protocols for semiconductor process chemicals including HF (TLV 0.5 ppm), silane (TLV 5 ppm, pyrophoric), arsine (TLV 0.005 ppm), and phosphine (TLV 0.3 ppm). Covers exposure monitoring, SDS requirements, and gas detection system design.

nodes.name: Ventilation & Exhaust Systems
nodes.description: Local exhaust ventilation (LEV), gas cabinets with dedicated exhaust, point-of-use abatement (thermal oxidizers, plasma, wet scrubbers), central scrubbing systems, and cleanroom air management for semiconductor fabs. Covers capture velocity design, duct material selection (PVDF for HF, 316L SS for toxic gases), scrubber chemistry, and exhaust monitoring.

nodes.name: Personal Protective Equipment
nodes.description: Respiratory protection (half-face, full-face, SCBA, supplied air), chemical-resistant glove selection (neoprene for HF, nitrile for solvents), eye/face protection, chemical suits, and cleanroom-compatible PPE. PPE selection matrices for HF handling, silane cylinder change, and arsine/phosphine work stations. Includes PPE program management and fit testing.

nodes.name: Emergency Response
nodes.description: Chemical-specific emergency procedures for HF exposure (calcium gluconate treatment), silane fire (gas supply shutdown, no water on burning silane), arsine inhalation (SCBA rescue, hemolysis monitoring), and phosphine release. Eyewash and deluge shower placement (10-second rule), spill kits, evacuation alarm tiers, and emergency equipment maintenance schedules.

nodes.name: Waste Management
nodes.description: Fluoride waste treatment (CaCl₂ precipitation to CaF₂), acid/base neutralization, heavy metal precipitation (hydroxide and sulfide methods, chromium VI reduction), solvent distillation recovery, TMAH biological degradation, and effluent monitoring with discharge limits (fluoride <10 ppm, Cu <2 ppm, As <0.1 ppm). Covers waste segregation, CaF₂ sludge dewatering, and source reduction strategies.

nodes.name: Toxicology
nodes.description: Identification, quantification, and management of toxic substances in industrial, pharmaceutical, and environmental contexts. Covers TLV-TWA exposure limits, LD50 data, antidote protocols, and biological monitoring.

nodes.name: Radiation Safety
nodes.description: Ionizing radiation protection: shielding design using half-value layers, dose monitoring with dosimetry, contamination control, and ALARA principles. Covers dose limits for occupational and public exposure.

nodes.name: Defense & Military Engineering
nodes.description: Weapons progression from stone to steel to firearms, fortification engineering from palisades to castles, armor systems from leather to plate, and siege warfare with mechanical artillery and logistics. Defense technology drives metallurgical advancement (weapons-grade steel), consumes industrial chemistry output (propellants, explosives), and reuses transport and knowledge infrastructure for logistics and command. Internal organizing axis: Chronological — capabilities ordered by materials era from stone-age weapons through iron-age siege engines to industrial-era gunpowder weapons.

nodes.name: Weapons & edged tools
nodes.description: Weapons progression from stone blades through bronze swords, iron-age weapons, steel swords and polearms, to black powder firearms (matchlock, flintlock, percussion cap). Blade hardness requirements: stone (Mohs 5-7), bronze (HB 80-120), wrought iron (HB 90-120), medium-carbon steel (HRC 45-55 for swords, HRC 58-62 for knife edges). Musket bore: 17.5-19 mm (.69-.75 caliber). Cross-reference chemistry/explosives.md for propellant compositions.

nodes.name: Fortification Engineering
nodes.description: Defensive construction from simple palisades and earthworks through stone curtain walls, moats, gatehouses, and castles to star forts and bastioned trace italienne. Wall dimensions: palisade (2-4 m height, 15-30 cm log diameter), earthen rampart (3-8 m height, 60° slope), stone curtain wall (6-12 m height, 1.5-3.0 m thickness), castle keep (15-30 m height, 2.5-4.0 m wall thickness). Cross-reference chemistry/cement.md for mortar.

nodes.name: Armor & Protective Systems
nodes.description: Personal and structural armor progression: leather (3-5 mm rawhide, ~30% cut resistance), scale and lamellar (overlapping bronze or iron plates 1-2 mm, 30-40% weight reduction vs plate), mail (riveted iron rings, 8-10 mm internal diameter, 1.5-2.0 mm wire gauge, defeats slashing), plate armor (1.5-3.0 mm tempered steel, ~20-25 kg full harness). Shield types and construction included. Cross-reference metals/iron-steel.md for heat treatment.

nodes.name: Siege Engineering & Military Logistics
nodes.description: Siege engines (battering rams, trebuchets, siege towers), mining and counter-mining, cannon artillery, and military logistics (supply chain management, field fortifications, baggage trains). Trebuchet counterweight: 5-15 tonnes for 100-300 kg projectile at 150-300 m range. Cannon bore progression: 50-200 mm by 15th century. Logistics: 1 kg grain and 4 L water per soldier per day minimum. Cross-reference transport domain for road/rail logistics.

nodes.name: Construction & Structural Engineering
nodes.description: Building construction from timber framing to reinforced concrete structures. Covers structural engineering (beams, columns, arches, trusses), building materials (timber, masonry, concrete), foundations for heavy industrial machinery, roofing, waterproofing, scaffolding, and hoisting systems. Construction enables all industrial domains — every factory, dam, bridge, and facility must be built.
nodes.early_win_note: Shelter and basic structures create immediate value — protected workspaces accelerate all other activities

nodes.name: Building Materials & Methods
nodes.description: Timber framing and joinery (mortise-tenon, dovetail, scarf joints), stone and masonry construction (ashlar, rubble, brick), roofing systems (timber trusses, slate, tile, metal), waterproofing (bitumen, lead flashing, cementitious coatings), and scaffolding/hoisting systems. The foundational building techniques from which all construction develops.

nodes.name: Structural Engineering
nodes.description: Structural analysis and design: beam theory (simply supported, cantilever, continuous), column buckling (Euler formula), arch and vault design, truss analysis (method of joints, method of sections), reinforced concrete design (beam and column sizing), steel connection design (riveted, bolted, welded), and foundation design. Includes specific load tables, material properties, and design formulas.

nodes.name: Industrial Buildings & Heavy Foundations
nodes.description: Factory building design for heavy industry: steel-frame and reinforced concrete industrial buildings, foundation design for heavy machinery (machine tools, forge hammers, stamping presses), vibration isolation (inertia blocks, isolation pads, spring mounts), floor loading (point loads, distributed loads, dynamic loads from machinery), crane runway design, and industrial roofing (clear spans, monitoring systems).

nodes.name: Food Processing
nodes.description: Industrial-scale transformation of raw agricultural output into stable, safe, transportable food products. Spans grain milling (stone to roller), food preservation (drying through refrigeration), dairy processing (separation, butter, cheese), and brewing/distilling (beer, wine, spirits). Food processing feeds the workforce that builds GPUs — without it, no specialist labor pool exists. Internal organizing axis: chronological, from stone-age techniques to industrial processes.
nodes.early_win_note: Flour from milling and preserved food from drying/salting create food surplus stability, enabling specialist labor division

nodes.name: Grain Milling
nodes.description: Progression from hand-powered stone grinding through water/wind-powered millstones to industrial roller milling. Flour production is the foundation of food processing — it converts stored grain into immediately usable food, dramatically improving calorie delivery efficiency and enabling bread, the staple workforce fuel.
nodes.early_win_note: Flour production multiplies grain utility by 2-3x — bread provides more calories per labor-hour than porridge

nodes.name: Food Preservation
nodes.description: Progression from drying and salting through smoking, fermentation, canning, pasteurization, to refrigeration. Food preservation eliminates seasonal starvation cycles and enables long-distance food transport, which directly enables urban workforce concentration and specialist labor.

nodes.name: Dairy Processing
nodes.description: Milk separation, butter churning, cheese making, and fermented dairy products. Converts highly perishable raw milk (spoils in hours at room temperature) into stable, transportable, protein-rich foods. Cheese is one of the most calorie-efficient preservation methods — 10 L milk yields ~1 kg cheese with months of shelf life.

nodes.name: Brewing & Distilling
nodes.description: Beer brewing (malted grain mash, fermentation, conditioning), wine making (grape crushing, fermentation, aging), and spirit distillation (pot still, column still). Brewing is simultaneously food preservation (beer was historically safer than water), calorie concentration, and the earliest industrial biotechnology. Distillation later provides industrial ethanol and feeds into chemistry.

nodes.name: Telecommunications
nodes.description: Long-distance communication systems spanning pre-electric signaling (semaphore, heliograph) through electrical telegraph, telephone, submarine cables, and radio. SIK boundary: shares <25% infrastructure overlap with transport — transport moves physical goods and people; telecom moves information. Railway block signaling is the notable intersection, covered by cross-reference to transport.telegraph. Internal organizing axis: Chronological — capabilities ordered by technological era from visual signaling through electronic radio.

nodes.name: Pre-Electric Communication
nodes.description: Visual and acoustic signaling systems for long-distance communication before electrical technology: signal fires, beacons, semaphore lines (Chappe telegraph), heliograph (mirror signaling), and flag signaling. Ranges from 5 km (signal fires) to 100+ km (heliograph). The first telecommunications infrastructure, enabling military coordination and emergency warning across distances impractical for couriers.

nodes.name: Electrical Telegraph Networks
nodes.description: Network-level telegraph systems: message routing, hub-and-spoke topology, duplex/quadruplex multiplexing, and network management. Hardware details (keys, sounders, batteries) covered by transport.telegraph — this node focuses on the telecommunications architecture that turns point-to-point circuits into information networks. Includes railway block signaling integration.

nodes.name: Telephone Systems
nodes.description: Voice communication over wire: Bell electromagnetic telephone, Edison carbon microphone, magneto ringers, manual switchboard (cord board) exchanges, and Strowger automatic step-by-step exchange (1889). The Strowger exchange eliminates human operators, enabling private dialing. Capacity: 100-10,000 subscriber lines per exchange. Loading coils extend effective range from 30 km to 150+ km.

nodes.name: Submarine Cable Systems
nodes.description: Undersea telegraph and telephone cables connecting continents. Gutta-percha insulation (natural latex from Palaquium gutta, dielectric strength 15-25 kV/mm), armored cable construction, cable-laying ships, deep-sea repair operations, and signal amplification. Bandwidth: 1-2 WPM on early transatlantic cables (1858), improving to 100+ WPM with mirror galvanometer and siphon recorder. The first global communications infrastructure.

nodes.name: Radio Communication
nodes.description: Wireless telegraphy and early voice radio: spark-gap transmitters (0.5-10 kW, 100-1000 kHz), crystal detectors (galena, carborundum), vacuum tube detectors and oscillators (Fleming valve 1904, de Forest Audion 1906), tuned circuits, antenna systems, and propagation fundamentals. Range: 50-500 km spark-gap, 100-3000 km with vacuum tube transmitters. Enables maritime communication, aviation communication, and broadcast.

nodes.name: Marine & Naval Engineering
nodes.description: Shipbuilding, marine propulsion, navigation, maritime infrastructure, and underwater engineering. Covers the engineering of vessels from dugout canoes to iron-hulled steamships, including hull construction methods, sailing rig evolution, marine engine development, harbor works, and submarine cable technology. Distinct from transport.shipping which covers water transport operations; marine covers design, construction, and maintenance of maritime systems.
nodes.early_win_note: Dugout canoes and small sailing vessels provide coastal transport, fishing, and trade access from Year 0-5, expanding the resource base and enabling contact with other settlements

nodes.name: Hull Construction & Shipbuilding
nodes.description: Progression of hull construction from dugout canoes through clinker-built, carvel-built, and iron/steel hulls. Covers structural design, fastening methods, watertight integrity, and the transition from wood to metal construction. Includes specific parameters for hull dimensions, plate thickness, frame spacing, and rivet specifications.

nodes.name: Marine Navigation
nodes.description: Navigation techniques from coastal piloting to celestial navigation and dead reckoning. Covers instrument development (compass, astrolabe, quadrant, sextant, chronometer), chart-making, and position determination. Includes instrument accuracy specifications and practical navigation calculations.

nodes.name: Marine Propulsion Systems
nodes.description: Evolution of marine propulsion from oar power through sail, steam, and diesel. Covers marine-specific engine adaptations (compound steam engines, triple-expansion engines, marine diesels), propeller design and efficiency, paddle wheels vs. screw propellers, and power-to-weight considerations for shipboard use.

nodes.name: Maritime Infrastructure & Underwater Engineering
nodes.description: Maritime infrastructure construction including harbors, breakwaters, dry docks, lighthouses, and piers. Also covers underwater cable laying (gutta-percha insulated telegraph cables), corrosion prevention (cathodic protection, anti-fouling), and marine salvage. Includes specific engineering parameters for all structures.
